titleStmt.title: Letter from William Martin to Edward Carson, 13 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Martin (1855-1933) to Edward Carson (1854-1935). Carson was a prominent Unionist Politician. Originally a practicing solicitor from Dublin, Carson's opposition to the proposed third Home Rule bill made him the figure head of Unionism throughout the island. He was the centerpiece of the speaking tour that culminated in Ulster Day (28 September 1912), when just under half a million signed Ulster's solemn league and covenant pledging to use ‘all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present conspiracy to set up a home rule parliament in Dublin’. Carson inaugurated the Larne gunrunning in 1914 which armed the UVF, many of whom Carson would encourage to join the British Army throughout the First World War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

correspAction.placeName: House of Commons, London, S.W., England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Hilden
Monaghan 13thJuly 1916 Dear Sir Edward Carson, I enclose you
copies of two letters, one from Major
Somerset Saunderson, and one from
Mr Blackley, Sub Sheriff of Cavan,
which I received yesterday. I called a
meeting of the Monaghan delegates to
the Ulster Unionist Council today,
and laid both letters before them, and
after considering the matter, they
passed a resolution, copy of which
I also enclose. I was directed to
send the correspondence and resolution
to you, in order that you may see
what is going on, if you are not
already aware of it. So far as
Monaghan is concerned, we will
have nothing to do with any going
back on the position decision already arrived
at, having complete confidence in you
as our leader. Yours faithfully Wm Martin The Right Hon
Sir Edward Carson
K.C. M.P.
House of Commons
London
